schools.summary: Gill: the apostle returns to particular exhortations, after having finished his general ones which chiefly concern patient suffering for Christ, and having particularly exhorted subjects to behave aright to civil magistrates, servants to their masters, and husbands and wives mutually, here addresses the elders; Peter exhorting the elders among them, calling himself also an elder, a witness of Christ's sufferings, and a partaker of the glory to be revealed — the apostle's humble appeal to the elders as a fellow-elder (Peter, turning to the pastors of the churches, exhorting them not from above as a lordly apostle but as one of themselves: 'the elders which are among you I exhort, who am also an elder,' placing himself alongside them in the same work, and naming two things that fit him to exhort: 'a witness of the sufferings of Christ,' one who saw and testifies of Christ's passion, 'and also a partaker of the glory that shall be revealed,' a sharer with them in the hope of the coming glory; the lowliness and warmth of this appeal, that Peter, though an apostle, comes as a fellow-elder, a fellow-witness of the cross, and a fellow-heir of glory, the better to win the elders to their duty).

themes: the elders which are among you I exhort, who am also an elder
a witness of the sufferings of Christ
also a partaker of the glory that shall be revealed
Peter appealing humbly as a fellow-elder

schools.summary: Gill: the phrase 'the flock of God which is among you' is descriptive of the flock to be fed, pointing it out and distinguishing it as God's own (not the pastors'), and 'feed' includes the whole work of the pastoral office, doing it in the best way they are capable of; Peter charging the elders to feed God's flock and take the oversight, not by constraint but willingly, not for filthy lucre but of a ready mind — the pastoral duty to feed and oversee the flock from pure motives (Peter giving the elders their great work and the spirit in which it must be done: 'feed the flock of God which is among you,' nourish and shepherd the people who are God's own flock not their own, 'taking the oversight thereof,' exercising the pastoral care and government, and marking the right motives by contrast with the wrong: 'not by constraint, but willingly,' not as a forced grudging task but gladly and freely, 'not for filthy lucre, but of a ready mind,' not for greed of base gain but with an eager devoted heart; the flock being God's, the elders but under-shepherds, and the work to be done with willing love not reluctance or covetousness).

themes: feed the flock of God which is among you, taking the oversight thereof
not by constraint, but willingly
not for filthy lucre, but of a ready mind
the elders but under-shepherds of God's flock

schools.summary: Gill: 'God's heritage,' or 'clergy' (the Greek word for a lot or inheritance, here meaning the whole people of God, his lot and inheritance, not the clergy in the later restricted sense), is not to be governed with tyranny, in a haughty, imperious, and arrogant manner; Peter charging that the elders not be lords over God's heritage, but be examples to the flock — the pastoral leadership by example, not domineering rule (Peter completing the manner of the eldership: 'neither as being lords over God's heritage,' not domineering, lording it over the people as petty tyrants, ruling by their own arbitrary will, for the flock is 'God's heritage,' his own inheritance and possession, not theirs to tyrannize, 'but being ensamples to the flock'; the better way of leading, that the true pastor governs not by overbearing authority but by being a pattern, a living example of all he teaches, drawing the flock to follow by going before them in faith, holiness, and love, so the elder leads as a shepherd and model, not as a lord).

themes: neither as being lords over God's heritage
but being ensamples to the flock
leadership by example, not domineering rule
the pastor a living pattern, going before the flock

schools.summary: Gill: by 'the chief Shepherd' is meant Christ, who may well be called so since he is God's fellow and in all respects equal with him, and is the great Shepherd over all the under-shepherds; this is the encouraging motive and argument to engage the elders and pastors to discharge their office faithfully, cheerfully, and humbly; Peter promising that when the chief Shepherd appears, they shall receive a crown of glory that fades not away — the unfading crown promised to faithful pastors at Christ's coming (Peter setting the great reward before the faithful elders: 'and when the chief Shepherd shall appear,' when Christ, the Chief Shepherd over all the pastors who are his under-shepherds, comes again in glory, 'ye shall receive a crown of glory that fadeth not away'; the beauty of the reward and its contrast with earthly crowns, that the faithful pastor shall be crowned not with a fading garland of leaves like the victors of old but with 'a crown of glory' that never withers or fades, so the elder who serves God's flock faithfully now, looking for no earthly gain, is encouraged by the sure and everlasting crown his Chief Shepherd will give at his appearing).

themes: and when the chief Shepherd shall appear
ye shall receive a crown of glory that fadeth not away
the unfading crown promised to faithful pastors
unlike the fading garlands of earthly victors

schools.summary: Gill: 'ye younger' are not younger officers (as if inferior officers to bishops were intended), for elders and pastors are the same, and there is no other office but that of deacons, but the younger members in age; a deference is to be paid by the younger to the elder, and proper respect shown; Peter charging the younger to submit to the elder, all to be subject one to another and clothed with humility, since God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble — the call to humility, with God's favour on the humble and resistance to the proud (Peter, having charged the elders, turning to the younger and to all: 'ye younger, submit yourselves unto the elder,' the younger to yield respect and submission, 'yea, all of you be subject one to another,' and all the members mutually in lowliness, 'and be clothed with humility,' put on humility as a garment that covers and adorns the whole life, and grounding it in God's own dealing: 'for God resisteth the proud, and giveth grace to the humble'; the weighty motive, that God sets himself against the proud, opposing and abasing them, but pours grace upon the humble, so humility is both commanded and crowned with God's favour, while pride is met by his resistance).

themes: ye younger, submit yourselves unto the elder
all of you be subject one to another, and be clothed with humility
for God resisteth the proud, and giveth grace to the humble
humility commanded and crowned with God's favour

schools.summary: Gill: 'humble yourselves,' or be ye humbled before God and in his sight; quietly submit to his will, patiently bear every affliction without murmuring, repining, or replying against him, be still under the rod and despise not the chastening of the Lord, mourn over sin as the cause, acknowledge your vileness and unworthiness, and stand in awe of God; Peter charging that they humble themselves under God's mighty hand, that he may exalt them in due time — the humble submission to God's hand that he may exalt in his own time (Peter drawing from God's dealing the practical charge: 'humble yourselves therefore under the mighty hand of God,' bow low and submit, especially under his afflicting chastening hand, owning his sovereignty and your own unworthiness, not resisting or murmuring, and adding the gracious promise that crowns such humility: 'that he may exalt you in due time'; the way up is down, that those who humble themselves under God's hand he will raise and exalt, but 'in due time,' in his own wise season not before, so the believer is to submit patiently to God's mighty ordering hand, trusting that the lowly shall be lifted up when God sees fit).

themes: humble yourselves therefore under the mighty hand of God
that he may exalt you in due time
humble submission under God's mighty hand
the way up is down, the lowly lifted up in God's time

schools.summary: Gill: the words are taken out of, or at least refer to, 'cast thy burden upon the Lord' (Psalm 55:22), where instead of 'cast thy burden' the Septuagint has 'cast thy care upon the Lord'; the care of the body and of all the affairs of life, concerning which saints should not be anxiously concerned; Peter charging that they cast all their care upon God, for he cares for them — the casting of all anxious care on the God who tenderly cares for his people (Peter, in the same breath as humbling under God's hand, giving the sweetest relief from anxiety: 'casting all your care upon him,' rolling the whole weight of every worry, burden, and anxious thought off your own heart and onto God, and giving the tender reason that makes this possible: 'for he careth for you'; the comfort, that the believer need not be crushed under the load of care, for he may cast it all, every care without exception, upon God, knowing that God himself cares for him with a Father's tender, watchful love, so humble submission and freedom from anxiety go together, both resting on the loving care of God).

themes: casting all your care upon him
for he careth for you
casting all anxious care on the caring God
God caring for his own with a Father's tender love

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle had exhorted to each of these before but thought fit to repeat them, sobriety and watchfulness being exceeding necessary and useful in the Christian life, and the one cannot well be without the other, for unless a man is sober in body and mind he will not be watchful, either over himself or others; Peter charging that they be sober and vigilant, because the devil, their adversary, walks about as a roaring lion seeking whom he may devour — the watchful sobriety needed against the prowling, devouring devil (Peter, having spoken of casting care on God, warning that the believer is not therefore to be careless of his soul: 'be sober, be vigilant,' self-controlled and watchful, awake and on guard, and giving the reason in a vivid and terrible image: 'because your adversary the devil,' the great enemy and accuser of the saints, 'as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour'; the picture of the danger, that the devil prowls like a hungry, roaring lion, restless and ravenous, ever seeking to destroy souls, so the believer, though he casts his care on God, must yet keep sober and watchful, knowing so fierce and tireless an enemy is ever near).

themes: be sober, be vigilant
because your adversary the devil, as a roaring lion, walketh about
seeking whom he may devour
watchful sobriety against the prowling devil

schools.summary: Gill: 'whom resist,' by no means giving way to him by indulging any sin or yielding to any temptation, but oppose him and stand against his wiles, his cunning and his power; Barnes: you are in no instance to yield to him but are in all forms to stand up and oppose him, and, feeble in yourselves, you are to confide in the aid of God; Peter charging that they resist the devil, steadfast in the faith, knowing the same afflictions are fulfilled in their brethren in the world — the steadfast resistance of the devil, with the comfort of shared suffering (Peter, against so fierce a foe, giving the believer's defence: 'whom resist stedfast in the faith,' do not flee or yield but stand firm against him, and that 'stedfast in the faith,' the firm grip of faith being the strength by which the devil is withstood, and adding a strengthening comfort: 'knowing that the same afflictions are accomplished in your brethren that are in the world'; the encouragement, that the believer's sufferings and conflict with the devil are not strange or solitary but the common lot of all the brethren throughout the world enduring the very same trials, so the saint resists the more steadfastly, knowing he does not suffer alone but shares the warfare of the whole church).

themes: whom resist stedfast in the faith
knowing that the same afflictions are accomplished in your brethren
that are in the world
resisting the devil, not suffering alone but sharing the church's warfare

schools.summary: Gill: 'the God of all grace' has riches of grace, an immense plenty of it in himself, has treasured up a fulness of grace in his Son, is the author of all the blessings of grace (electing, adopting, justifying, pardoning, regenerating grace), and is the giver of the several graces of the Spirit (faith, hope, love, repentance); Peter praying that the God of all grace, who called them to eternal glory by Christ, would, after they have suffered a while, perfect, establish, strengthen, and settle them — the God of all grace perfecting and establishing the sufferers after a little suffering (Peter ending his exhortations with a rich prayer for the suffering saints, addressing God by a glorious title and a sure ground of hope: 'the God of all grace,' the fountain of every grace, 'who hath called us unto his eternal glory by Christ Jesus,' whose calling is unto eternal glory through Christ, and setting the brief trial against the eternal end: 'after that ye have suffered a while,' the suffering being short, 'a while' only, and praying a fourfold blessing: 'make you perfect,' fully fit and complete you, 'stablish,' settle you firm, 'strengthen,' give you might, 'settle you,' ground you on a sure foundation; the comfort, that the God of all grace himself will, after the brief suffering, do all this for his people).

themes: but the God of all grace, who hath called us unto his eternal glory by Christ Jesus
after that ye have suffered a while
make you perfect, stablish, strengthen, settle you
God perfecting and establishing the sufferers

schools.summary: Gill: glory is due to God for all the grace bestowed; the Syriac version begins this doxology in the preceding verse, reading 'to the God of grace... who hath called us,' connecting them with 'be glory, and power, and honour' for ever; Peter ascribing glory and dominion to God for ever and ever, sealed with Amen — the closing doxology to the God of all grace (Peter, overflowing from the prayer just made, breaking into a doxology to the God of all grace who does such great things for his people: 'to him be glory and dominion for ever and ever,' ascribing to God the 'glory,' the honour and praise due to him for all his grace, and the 'dominion,' the power and sovereign rule that are his, and that not for a time but 'for ever and ever,' world without end, and sealing it with 'Amen'; the fitness of the close, that having shown the God of all grace as the believer's perfecter and establisher, Peter ends by giving him all the glory and owning his everlasting dominion, as is most due).

themes: to him be glory and dominion for ever and ever
Amen
the closing doxology to the God of all grace
ascribing glory and sovereign rule, world without end

schools.summary: Gill: Silvanus is the same with Silas, so often mentioned in the Acts of the Apostles as a companion of the Apostle Paul, whom Peter met with in his travels and sent this letter by him, or used him as his amanuensis, or both; his character is that he was 'a faithful brother' to those to whom this letter was written; Peter saying that by Silvanus, a faithful brother, he has written briefly, exhorting and testifying that this is the true grace of God in which they stand — the bearer of the epistle and Peter's aim, to exhort and confirm them in the true grace (Peter naming the faithful messenger and summing up his whole purpose: he has written 'by Silvanus, a faithful brother,' Silas the trusted carrier or scribe, and 'briefly,' a short letter considering its weighty matter, and his twofold design was 'exhorting, and testifying that this is the true grace of God wherein ye stand'; the aim of the whole epistle, both to exhort them to their duties and to bear solemn witness that the doctrine and grace they had received and stood fast in was indeed 'the true grace of God,' the genuine gospel, so Peter's closing word confirms them against all doubt, assuring them they stand in the real grace of God).

themes: by Silvanus, a faithful brother unto you, I have written briefly
exhorting, and testifying that this is the true grace of God
wherein ye stand
confirming them in the genuine gospel against doubt

schools.summary: Gill: the word 'church' is supplied (as in the Vulgate, Syriac, and Arabic); some by 'Babylon' understand Rome, so called figuratively in Revelation, an ancient opinion held by Papias as Eusebius relates, but whether Peter was at Rome when he wrote is uncertain, so Babylon may be the literal city (where a Jewish population dwelt, a natural place for the apostle to the circumcision); 'Marcus my son' is John Mark; Peter conveying the greeting of the church at Babylon, elected together with them, and of Mark his son — the salutation from a fellow-elect church and from Mark (Peter sending the loving greetings of the believers where he was: 'the church that is at Babylon, elected together with you, saluteth you,' the fellow-church chosen of God as they were, 'and so doth Marcus my son'; the debate over 'Babylon,' whether the literal city or figuratively Rome, and 'Marcus my son' being John Mark, Peter's spiritual son or close companion, so the letter closes with the warm fellowship of the churches and the bond of the gospel's family).

sides.position: The literal city of Babylon, where a Jewish population dwelt — a natural place for Peter, apostle to the circumcision
Rome, called Babylon figuratively (as in Revelation) — an ancient opinion held by Papias and others

themes: the church that is at Babylon, elected together with you, saluteth you
and so doth Marcus my son
the salutation from a fellow-elect church and Mark
debate whether Babylon is the literal city or Rome

schools.summary: Gill: the 'kiss of charity' (the Vulgate, Syriac, and Arabic reading 'an holy kiss,' as elsewhere) intends such a kiss as is not only opposite to everything lascivious and impure but is expressive of true love and affection, and is hearty and sincere; Peter charging that they greet one another with a kiss of charity, and closing with the benediction, 'Peace be with you all that are in Christ Jesus. Amen' — the parting call to brotherly love and the blessing of peace on all in Christ (Peter closing the epistle as he had filled it, with love and peace: 'greet ye one another with a kiss of charity,' let the believers express their mutual love by the customary holy greeting, the token of sincere brotherly affection, and ending with the benediction: 'Peace be with you all that are in Christ Jesus. Amen'; the fitness of the close, that a letter written to suffering, scattered saints ends with the invoking of 'peace,' the deep peace of God, upon 'all that are in Christ Jesus,' every true believer united to him, so Peter's last word to the persecuted church is the gift of peace in Christ, sealed with 'Amen').

themes: greet ye one another with a kiss of charity
Peace be with you all that are in Christ Jesus. Amen
the parting call to brotherly love and benediction of peace
the gift of peace in Christ to the persecuted church
